sections.heading: Prerequisites
sections.body:
To enable customer-managed keys with AKV (Azure Key Vault) for a MongoDB project, you
must have:

- An M10 or larger cluster.

- The Azure (Microsoft Azure) account, and the encryption key in your AKV (Azure Key Vault).

  To learn how to configure these Azure components, see the
  [Azure Documentation](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/index).

  Atlas uses these resources when enabling encryption at rest
  for a cluster in the Atlas project.

- Permissions in Azure (Microsoft Azure) to:

  - Register an enterprise Service Principal in your tenant.
    This prerequisite requires Microsoft Entra ID permissions to add or
    manage Enterprise Applications, such as Application Administrator,
    Cloud Application Administrator, or Global Administrator.

    To learn more, see [Quickstart: Add an enterprise application](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/identity/enterprise-apps/add-application-portal).

  - Access to the Key Vault key using one of the following
    options:

    - [Azure RBAC](access-azure-rbac) (at the Key Vault or key
      scope)

    - [Key Vault access policies](access-azure-policies)

- Project Owner access in Atlas for the project for which you
  want to enable Encryption at Rest.

sections.heading: Considerations
sections.body:
To use a customer-managed key stored in AKV (Azure Key Vault) for EAR, the
Atlas-managed Service Principal must have permission to perform
cryptographic operations on the specific Key Vault key.

The required key permissions include:

- `get`

- `encrypt`

- `decrypt`

You should scope permissions as narrowly as possible and grant them
using one of the following options:

- [Azure RBAC](access-azure-rbac) (at the Key Vault or key scope)

- [Key Vault access policies](access-azure-policies)

To learn more, see [Quickstart: Add an enterprise application](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/identity/enterprise-apps/add-application-portal).

sections.heading: Set Up EAR AKV (Azure Key Vault) Secretless Authentication
sections.body:
To configure Encryption at Rest (EAR) with secretless authentication,
you must:

- Create an Atlas-managed Azure (Microsoft Azure) Service Principal.

- Grant the Service Principal access to your Key Vault key.

- Configure EAR to use the Service Principal.

sections.heading: Create an Atlas-managed Azure (Microsoft Azure) Service Principal
sections.body:
Create an Azure (Microsoft Azure) Service Principal in your tenant for the
Atlas-owned Azure (Microsoft Azure) application and register it with Atlas
using the Cloud Provider Access API. Atlas returns a `roleId`
that you use when you enable EAR.

Atlas uses the following Azure (Microsoft Azure) application identity:

```sh
atlasAzureAppId: 9efedfcc-2eca-4b27-a613-0cad1e114cb7
```

sections.heading: Step 1: Get your  tenant ID.
sections.body:
Run the following command to retrieve the tenant ID for the
resource in which the Service Principal will exist:

```sh
az account show --query tenantId -o tsv
```

sections.heading: Step 2: Create or locate the  Service Principal.
sections.body:
If a Service Principal already exists for the Atlas
application, run the following command to locate it:

```sh
az ad sp list --filter "appId eq '9efedfcc-2eca-4b27-a613-0cad1e114cb7'"
```

If it doesn't exist, run the following command to create it:

```sh
az ad sp create --id 9efedfcc-2eca-4b27-a613-0cad1e114cb7
```

From the output, copy the `id` field, which is the Service
Principal Object ID that Atlas requires.

sections.heading: Step 3: Register the Service Principal with .
sections.body:
Use the [Cloud Provider Access API](atlas/reference/api-resources-spec/v2):

**Endpoint:**

```sh
POST /api/atlas/v2/groups/{groupId}/cloudProviderAccess
```

**Request body example:**

```sh
{
  "providerName": "AZURE",
  "tenantId": "<azure-tenant-id>",
  "servicePrincipalId": "<service-principal-object-id>",
  "atlasAzureAppId": "9efedfcc-2eca-4b27-a613-0cad1e114cb7"
}
```

Atlas provisions and manages the Service Principal and
returns an `id` value, which is the `roleId` required when
you [configure EAR](config-ear-secretless).

sections.heading: Grant AKV (Azure Key Vault) Permissions
sections.body:
Authorize the Atlas-managed Service Principal to perform
cryptographic operations on the Key Vault key used for EAR.

You can grant access using either Azure RBAC or Key Vault access
policies.

sections.heading: Grant Access Using Azure (Microsoft Azure) RBAC
sections.body: To grant access using Azure (Microsoft Azure) RBAC:

sections.heading: Step 1: Assign the Reader role so  can read Key Vault metadata.
sections.body:
Run the following command:

```sh
az role assignment create \
  --assignee-object-id "$SERVICE_PRINCIPAL_OBJECT_ID" \
  --assignee-principal-type ServicePrincipal \
  --role "Reader" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/$SUBSCRIPTION_ID/resourceGroups/$RESOURCE_GROUP/providers/Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/$KEY_VAULT_NAME"
```

sections.heading: Step 2: Assign a cryptographic role such as Key Vault Crypto User.
sections.body:
Run the following command:

```sh
az role assignment create \
  --assignee-object-id "$SERVICE_PRINCIPAL_OBJECT_ID" \
  --assignee-principal-type ServicePrincipal \
  --role "Key Vault Crypto User" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/$SUBSCRIPTION_ID/resourceGroups/$RESOURCE_GROUP/providers/Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/$KEY_VAULT_NAME"
```

> **Note:** You can also use a custom role, as long as it grants
> equivalent key permissions.

codeBlocks.content:
az role assignment create \
  --assignee-object-id "$SERVICE_PRINCIPAL_OBJECT_ID" \
  --assignee-principal-type ServicePrincipal \
  --role "Key Vault Crypto User" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/$SUBSCRIPTION_ID/resourceGroups/$RESOURCE_GROUP/providers/Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/$KEY_VAULT_NAME"

sections.heading: Grant Access Using Key Vault Access Policies
sections.body:
If your Key Vault uses access policies, run the following command to
grant key permissions directly:

```sh
az keyvault set-policy \
  --subscription "$SUBSCRIPTION_ID" \
  --resource-group "$RESOURCE_GROUP" \
  --name "$KEY_VAULT_NAME" \
  --object-id "$SERVICE_PRINCIPAL_OBJECT_ID" \
  --key-permissions get encrypt decrypt wrapKey unwrapKey
```

codeBlocks.content:
az keyvault set-policy \
  --subscription "$SUBSCRIPTION_ID" \
  --resource-group "$RESOURCE_GROUP" \
  --name "$KEY_VAULT_NAME" \
  --object-id "$SERVICE_PRINCIPAL_OBJECT_ID" \
  --key-permissions get encrypt decrypt wrapKey unwrapKey

sections.heading: Configure Encryption at Rest to Use Secretless Authentication
sections.body:
After you register and authorize the Service Principal in Azure (Microsoft Azure),
update your EAR configuration with the `roleId` that the
[Cloud Provider Access API](atlas/reference/api-resources-spec/v2) returned.

Atlas UI

Atlas Admin API

After this update, Atlas authenticates to AKV (Azure Key Vault) using short-lived
Azure (Microsoft Azure) AD tokens instead of static credentials.

sections.heading: Configure Encryption at Rest to Use Secretless Authentication
sections.body:
Atlas supports two authentication methods for AKV (Azure Key Vault):

- **Service Principal (Recommended)**: Use an Atlas-managed
  service principal specific to your project to authenticate to
  AKV (Azure Key Vault) (secretless authentication covered here).

- **Static Credentials**: Provide customer-managed credentials.
  To learn more, see [Manage Customer Keys with Azure Key Vault Over a Public Network](security-azure-kms-public-network) or
  [Manage Customer Keys with Azure Key Vault Over Private Endpoints](security-azure-kms-pvt-endpoint).

| Subscription ID     | Enter the Subscription ID of the Key Vault.                                                                                       |
| ------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Resource Group Name | Enter the Resource Group name of an Azure Resource Group containing the Key Vault.                                                |
| Key Vault Name      | Enter the name of the Key Vault. Ensure that the Key Vault has the necessary Access Policies. To learn more, see Required Access. |

> **Note:** You can't modify the AKV (Azure Key Vault) configuration here after you
> [Enable and Set up Private Endpoint Connections for a Project](azure-kms-enable-pvt-endpoint) to your AKV (Azure Key Vault).

| Key Identifier | Enter the full URL (Uniform Resource Locator) for the key created in the Key Vault.IMPORTANT: The key identifier must be provided in the full Azure general format:https\://\{keyvault-name}.vault.azure.net/\{object-type}/\{object-name}/\{object-version} |
| -------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |

To learn more, see [Enable and Set up Private Endpoint Connections for a Project](azure-kms-enable-pvt-endpoint)

If you configured Atlas using the Atlas Administration API to
communicate with AKV (Azure Key Vault) using Azure Private Link to ensure that all traffic
between Atlas and Key Vault takes place over Azure (Microsoft Azure)'s
private network interfaces, Atlas sets the Require
Private Networking status to Active. If the status is
Inactive, you can, optionally, complete the steps to
[Enable and Set up Private Endpoint Connections for a Project](azure-kms-enable-pvt-endpoint) if you want Atlas to use
private endpoint connections for your AKV (Azure Key Vault).

Atlas displays a banner in the Atlas console during the
encryption process.

sections.heading: Configure Encryption at Rest to Use Secretless Authentication
Migrate from Static Credentials
sections.body:
If your project already uses static credentials (`tenantId`,
`clientId`, `secret`), you can migrate without cluster downtime.

- Create an Atlas-managed Service Principal.

- Grant Key Vault permissions.

- Patch the EAR configuration with the new `roleId`.

If validation fails, Atlas continues using static credentials.

If validation succeeds, Atlas removes the stored static
credentials, and the `roleId` becomes the source of truth for
authentication.

If your cluster uses Private Networking, Atlas validates Key Vault
access asynchronously from the data nodes.

- Validation is not completed immediately when the `PATCH` request is
  accepted.

- Data nodes validate Key Vault access after the configuration is
  applied.

Before you patch EAR with `roleId`, ensure all Key Vault permissions
are correctly configured. Misconfigured access can result in validation
failures on data nodes, which may eventually disrupt EAR operations and
lead to cluster shutdown.

Before you patch EAR, verify that your Service Principal has the
required Key Vault permissions.

sections.heading: Verify Azure RBAC Role Assignments
sections.body:
Run the following command:

```sh
az role assignment list \
  --assignee "$SERVICE_PRINCIPAL_OBJECT_ID" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/$SUBSCRIPTION_ID/resourceGroups/$RESOURCE_GROUP/providers/Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/$KEY_VAULT_NAME"
```

Confirm the assigned role grants key permissions such as `get`,
`encrypt`, and `decrypt`.

sections.heading: Verify Key Vault Access Policies
sections.body:
Run the following command:

```sh
az keyvault show \
  --name "$KEY_VAULT_NAME" \
  --resource-group "$RESOURCE_GROUP" \
  --query "properties.accessPolicies"
```

Confirm the Service Principal is listed and includes the required key
permissions.
